preamble: The elision of the glottal fricative [h] as a final Laryngeal Coda is the definitive stage in the formation of a falling Pitch Contour in many East Asian languages. According to Haudricourt's model of Tonogenesis, the loss of final [h] (often derived from an earlier [s]) phonemicized the pitch lowering effect on the preceding vowel.
phoneticEffects: Elision, Tonogenesis, Pitch Fall, Voice Loss, Coda Deletion, Falling Tone

examples.note: Following the debuccalization of *s to [h], the eventual loss of this glottal fricative resulted in the development of the 'Sắc' tone (falling) in Vietnamese.
Final stage where the laryngeal coda disappears, leaving the complex falling tones.
In Sinitic historical phonology, the 'Departing' (Qù sheng) tone arose from the loss of a final glottal fricative [h].
The departing tone stabilizes as the final [h] is elided.
Thai and other Tai-Kadai languages exhibit a falling tone register associated with the historical elision of a final glottal coda.

Proto-Mongolic had a word-initial *h- that was systematically lost in the development of most daughter languages including Khalkha and Buryat. Only peripheral Mongolic languages (e.g., Monguor/Tu) preserve reflexes of this initial *h- (sometimes as /f/ via Proto-Altaic *p). E.g., Proto-Mongolic *hon- > Khalkha on- (to enter). This deletion is reconstructed by Janhunen as one of the earliest post-Proto-Mongolic changes.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Thurgood, G. (2002). From Ancient Southeast Asian Tonal Systems to Today.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
